papers.title_en: UniSteer: Text-Guided Flow Matching in Activation Space for Versatile LLM Steering
papers.authors: Yingdong Shi, Ruiming Zhang, Changming Li, Zhiyu Yang, Kaixing Zhang, Jingyi Yu, Kan Ren
papers.evidence: 文本引导的激活流匹配用于推理时引导LLM
papers.tldr: 现有激活操控方法依赖固定方向或任务专用模块，难以适应细粒度概念和组合约束。UniSteer通过学习文本条件激活速度场，利用流反转将源激活在目标文本条件下再生并注入LLM。在三个LLM上统一支持行为控制、真实性、细粒度概念、多约束指令和激活分类。该方法提供了通用文本引导操控框架，显著增强了灵活性和可扩展性。
papers.abstract_en: Activation-based control steers large language models (LLMs) by intervening on their internal representations during inference, and has emerged as an effective paradigm for controlling behaviors such as persona and style. However, existing methods often rely on fixed steering directions or task-specific intervention modules, making them difficult to adapt to fine-grained concepts and compositional constraints. We propose UniSteer, a text-guided activation flow matching model that learns a conditional distribution over residual-stream activations from natural-language conditions. Instead of fitting a separate intervention for each target behavior, UniSteer learns a universal conditional velocity field in activation space. At inference time, UniSteer performs flow inversion by partially transporting a source activation toward a latent state and regenerating it under a target textual condition before injecting it back into the frozen LLM. The same conditional model supports activation-space classification by selecting the textual label with the lowest reconstruction energy. Experiments on three target LLMs show that UniSteer provides a unified interface across behavioral control, truthfulness steering, fine-grained concept steering, multi-constraint instruction following, and activation-space classification.

papers.title_en: When Context Flips, Safety Breaks: Diagnosing Brittle Safety in Aligned Language Models
papers.evidence: 诊断对齐大语言模型的安全脆弱性，直接关联安全退化问题
papers.tldr: 对齐语言模型在安全基准测试中表现良好，但情境变化会导致安全动作变为有害，即脆弱安全。本文提出context-flip评估方法，测试12个模型发现所有模型均存在安全-常识差距，平均增加17.4个百分点。模型尽管感知到情境变化，仍因策略覆盖而非理解失败而输出有害内容。标准动作级护栏在灾难性后果翻转场景中完全失效，状态感知验证器可正确拦截。研究揭示了动作级内容审核的系统性盲点，推动状态感知架构替代。
papers.abstract_en: Safety benchmark scores provide incomplete evidence of deployment readiness: aligned language models often adhere to rigid rules even when a situational update flips which action is safe. We term this failure brittle safety. To diagnose it, we introduce context-flip evaluation, testing 12 models across a safety benchmark (PacifAIst) and two commonsense controls using paired variants where the nominally safe action produces harm. Three findings emerge. First, brittle safety is safety-specific: all 12 models exhibit a safety-commonsense gap (mean +17.4 pp). Baseline accuracy fails to predict brittleness: among models above 90% baseline accuracy, brittleness rates range from 13.7% to 90.0%. Second, failures stem from policy override rather than miscomprehension: despite acknowledging the context change in every case, models persist via three distinct mechanisms that vary by update type and model family. Third, on a hand-audited probe of catastrophic consequence-flip scenarios, standard action-level guardrails catch none, while a state-aware validator catches all without false alarms on correct interventions. This indicates that action-level content moderation is systematically blind to consequence-flips, motivating state-aware architectural alternatives. We release our protocol, perturbed benchmarks, and deployment probe.
